misconceptionSummary: Students write 10 × n instead of 10ⁿ — treating the power as ordinary multiplication.

lines.text: Treated the exponent as a multiplier

steps.narration: The student multiplied ten by four instead of raising ten to the power four. Ten times four is forty — completely different from ten to the power four, which is ten thousand.
Ten times four and ten to the power four are entirely different operations. A power means the base is multiplied by itself that many times — not multiplied by the exponent.
Count the zeros in ten thousand: four zeros. Write ten with four as the exponent. The exponent is the zero count, nothing else.
Ten to the power four equals ten thousand. Count the zeros — use that as the exponent. Never multiply ten by the exponent.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: calculating 10 × 4 = 40 when asked for 10⁴. A power means repeated multiplication: 10⁴ = 10 × 10 × 10 × 10 = 10 000.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: 10⁴ does not mean 10 × 4. It means 10 multiplied by itself 4 times. Count the zeros in the number — that is the exponent.
